Assignment For Session 6
The Snap Suggestion


1. Create your own Snap Suggestion and write it one sentence at a time (followed by an ellipsis, or …) within your PowerPoint or Keynote template, using the formula below (Go to 3XVSLFormula.com/member, login, and go to The 5-Step VSL Process menu option. Then, click on the Downloads tab to access these templates.)

The Snap Suggestion
Optional Introduction
Pattern Interrupt
The Big Promise
Video Scarcity
Something Completely Different
The "Let's Make A Deal" Transition

Part 1: Optional Introduction
Assuming you are the 'face' of your product/service, simply enter your introduction below (Example: "Hi, I'm Jon…)





Part 2: Pattern Interrupt
Create both an image interrupt and a verbal interrupt using the formulas below:

(See PDF Template for examples of pattern interrupts…)

Image Interrupt Exercise

1. Write down 5 objects or elements from your Nightmare Story that have nothing to do with the product/service you are selling. My examples from my Nightmare Story were:
	• A car
	• A jacket
	• Prostitutes
	• A store
	• Doughnuts

2. Select the object or element you will use for a picture or drawing to create an image interrupt. I selected a jacket for my pattern interrupt image. Reference the video slides (under Downloads tab) to see why.

3. Reference your object to the number 1 desire of your reader. (Example:  This is a jacket, and in a few minutes you'll see why this jacket holds the secret to your weight loss…) You will open a loop when you do this, and you'll close that loop within your Nightmare Story.

Verbal Interrupt Exercise

1. Using the Driving Force behind your Prime Benefit in your USP, write down the primary thing your viewer will discover by watching your video sales letter. You can do this within your PowerPoint or Keynote file and select the pattern interrupt you want to go with when your VSL is finished. 

Example: My primary benefit from my USP was "eat your favorite foods and lose weight." My driving force, or the reason this benefit WORKS, was a coined phrase:  Caloric Variation (or Staggering). 

With this information, my verbal interrupt was created by creating mystery and 'wow' around this driving force. You do not come out and say what the driving force is-rather, you hint at it with wordsmithing.

Example:  "You're about to discover one really old and kinda weird fat loss trick that I've used for years..."  Note: The "trick" is the Caloric Staggering Principle. 

Create this within your VSL template using one sentence per slide (Note:  A sentence can include multiple ellipses, as the example above indicates. Just be sure that your entire slide is not all text. 

TIP: After 40 words, you should almost always go to a new slide. Also, slides can be very short (10-15 words). 

Part 3: The Big Promise
First, enter your suggestion. This is simply a request to watch your presentation all the way to the end. 

Example:  "Watch this presentation until the end, and you will also discover…"

The last line leads into the Promise. Your promise is merely several benefits from your 3 Tips exercise with 2-3 "withouts" included.

Example: "…how you can burn more fat in a single day than you have in the past 15 days... without starving yourself silly or doing any cardio whatsoever!...

I used just an element from one of my 3 Tips, and followed it by including two "without" statements. So, it's "Something awesome WITHOUT something negative, or something negative, or something negative!"
Part 4: Video Scarcity
Next, let the viewer know that your video will not be online forever.

Examples:
"I don't know how long I'll have this online..." 
"I don't know how long this will be free..."
Best option:  "I have no idea how long I'll keep this presentation online... or how long it will be free of charge to watch... so watch this now, while you still can..."

Part 5: Something Completely Different
Here, you simply remind your reader that this is unlike anything they've seen before. 

Example: 

"You may think you have tried it all and seen it all, but you have never seen anything like this before..."

"...and I promise: These Tips and solutions will shock (or surprise, astonish, stun) you..."

Note:  This is written over two slides.

Part 6: "Let's Make A Deal" Transition
This transitions the viewer into Step 2 of the 5-Step VSL Process, The Vital Connection.

Step 1: Reinforce Your Promise
Here you begin with the word "Remember:", and remind the user of what you'll be sharing in a big picture sense (nothing specific). 

Example:  "Remember, I'm about to share some awesome weight loss info with you... and even more little-known tips to help you lose weight even faster…"
Step 2: Open The Nightmare Loop
You now move the reader into an open loop (the promise opens the loop, which you will close during your 3 tips) and introduce the reader to your Nightmare Story.

Example:  "First, I'm going to share a story with you that's pretty embarrassing... you'll see why in just a moment, so keep watching..."

Conclude with one simple word:  "Deal?"  So my final example reads:

"First, I'm going to share a story with you that's pretty embarrassing... you'll see why in just a moment, so keep watching...deal?"
